html:
Jürgen von Farensbach, born in 1551 and died in 1602, was a military figure of German-Baltic origin. He was the father of Georg Wolmar von Fahrensbach.

After serving in various armies, including the Swedish army, Farensbach entered Danish service and was appointed governor of Ösel (Saaremaa) by Frederick II. He held this position for some time after entering Polish service in 1580, but eventually fully joined Poland. He followed Sigismund during his military campaigns in Sweden and participated in the Battle of Stångebro.

== Sources == Svensk uppslagsbok (Swedish Encyclopedia). Malmö, 1932.

How certain are we about this?

From the Vasa era onwards, individuals are well-documented in archives. The uncertainty lies more in interpretation and how later generations perceived them – especially since those in power themselves played a role in shaping historical narratives.
tldr: During the 16th century, Jürgen von Farensbach was a military figure who served in the armies of various countries, including Sweden and Poland.
lattlast:
Jürgen von Farensbach was born in 1551 and died in 1602. He came from a region that today lies within Germany and the Baltic countries. He was a soldier who served under various kings throughout his life.

He initially served in the Swedish army before beginning work for Denmark. There, he obtained a high position on the island of Ösel (Saaremaa), which is an island in the Baltic Sea. Later, he switched sides and began working for Poland.

During his time in Polish service, he participated in wars against Sweden. He was involved in several battles, including the major Battle of Stångebro.
